question.text: A mutual fund distributor creates a social media post stating: "Invest in XYZ Equity Fund — it has given 45% returns in the last 6 months! Guaranteed to double your money in 2 years!" Which SEBI Advertisement Code violations are present in this post?

answer.text: Only the guarantee claim violates the code; showing 6-month returns is acceptable
Only showing past performance for a non-standard period is a violation
Multiple violations: (1) guarantee/assured returns claim, (2) non-standard performance period (6 months instead of 1/3/5 years), (3) no mandatory risk disclaimer, (4) no benchmark comparison
There are no violations as distributors are exempt from the Advertisement Code

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** This social media post has multiple SEBI Advertisement Code violations: (1) "Guaranteed to double your money" — guaranteeing or assuring returns is strictly prohibited; (2) "45% returns in the last 6 months" — performance must be shown for standardized periods (1, 3, 5 years, since inception) on CAGR basis, not cherry-picked periods; (3) No mandatory disclaimer "Mutual Fund investments are subject to market risks..."; (4) No benchmark (TRI) comparison alongside the performance claim. Distributors ARE covered by the Advertisement Code — they are not exempt.
**The Trap:** Some candidates may identify only one violation when multiple exist. The most dangerous trap is Option D — distributors are absolutely NOT exempt from the Advertisement Code. Any person involved in the distribution or marketing of mutual funds must comply with SEBI's advertising guidelines.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Advertisement Code violations are scenario-based questions common in Series V-A and V-B. Series XXIV tests compliance monitoring of advertisements. Series III-C tests similar advertising restrictions for investment advisers. Social media posts are specifically covered under SEBI's advertising framework.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Social media is a powerful marketing tool, but every post about mutual funds is considered an "advertisement" under SEBI rules. Before posting, check: (1) no guaranteed return claims, (2) standard performance periods with CAGR, (3) TRI benchmark comparison, (4) mandatory disclaimer included. Several distributors have faced AMFI/SEBI action for non-compliant social media posts.
